Description: Structured decision-support process for assessing climate impacts, evaluating management goals, and developing conservation strategies for changing conditions.
Developer/ Credits: USDA Northern Forests Climate Hub and the Northern Institute of Applied Climate Science, a multi-institutional partnership led by USFS
How applied?: Managers and landowners could use this workbook to develop and implement climate-informed management actions, including habitat management, restoration, and planning for future conditions.
Related Publications - 1: Swanston, Chris; Janowiak, Maria, eds. 2016. Forest Adaptation Resources: Climate change tools and approaches for land managers, 2nd Edition. Gen. Tech. Rep. NRS-87-2. Newtown Square, PA: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Northern Research Station.161 p.
Related Publications - 2: Janowiak, Maria K.; Swanston, Christopher W.; Nagel, Linda M.; Brandt, Leslie A.; Butler, Patricia R.; Handler, Stephen D.; Shannon, P. Danielle; Iverson, Louis R.; Matthews, Stephen N.; Prasad, Anantha; Peters, Matthew P. 2014. A practical approach for translating climate change adaptation principles into forest management actions. Journal of Forestry. 112(5): 424-433.
Related Publications - 3: Janowiak, M.; Dostie, D.; Wilson, M.; Kucera, M.; Skinner, R.H.; Hatfield, J.L.; Hollinger, D.; Swanston, C. 2016. Adaptation Resources for Agriculture: Responding to Climate Variability and Change in the Midwest and Northeast. Washington, DC: U.S. Department of Agriculture. 70 p.

Tool Name: Burned Area Emergency Response Treatments Catalog
Description: BAER (Burned Area Emergency Response) treatments catalog that includes various postfire treatments for land, channels, roads, and protection and savety.
Full Citation: Napper C. (2006). Burned area emergency response treatments catalog (pp. 0625-1801). US Forest Service, San Dimas Technology and Development Center.
Developer/ Credits: Napper, US Forest Service, San Dimas Technology and Development Center.
How applied?: A catalog of treatments performed under BAER

Description: An interagency portal that provides comprehensive access to federal burn severity mapping data and field assessment data.
Developer/ Credits: USDA, USFS, DOI, USGS
How applied?: Used for emergency stabilization, rehabilitation planning, vegetation recovery assessment, restoration prioritization, wildfire effects monitoring, and evaluation of fire patterns and trends over time.

Description: A platform that uses fire/atmosphere models to simulate fire behavior and support prescribed fire planning and risk assessment.
Developer/ Credits: WIFIRE
How applied?: Used to prioritize fuel treatments, plan prescribed burns, simulate fire behavior scenarios, and evaluate risks and tradeoffs in fire management decisions.

Description: Knowledge-sharing platform that provides access to a digital library of climate change adaptation case studies, tools, and resources.
Developer/ Credits: EcoAdapt
How applied?: Used to support climate adaptation planning and implementation by providing access to case studies, tools, and resources that inform assessment, planning, implementation, and evaluation of adaptation strategies, and to facilitate learning and knowledge exchange across sectors.

Description: Web-based tool that helps ecosystem managers identify seed sources best adapted to local climate conditions at their planting sites. Intended to be used after Post-fire Conifer Regeneration Planning Tool and Post-fire Reforestation Success Estimation Tool.
Region or Habitat: CA, OR, WA, ID, & NV. Some features limited to CA. Some focus on Sierra Nevada forests, CA
Developer/ Credits: Stewart, J., Zhao, Y., with Wright, J., O'Neill, G. Stewart J. affiliation: UC Davis. Funders: CalFire; University of California; USFS Pacific Southwest Research Station.
How applied?: Managers could use this tool to select best seed sources to maximize tree growth based on expected decrease in future growth with warming climate.

Description: An index of burn severity developed to assess fire effects on vegetation and soil. Composite burn index (CBI) plots rate the burn severity for five categories of vegetation and soil. An overAllCBI index can be calculated based on these five ratings. This index facilitates comparison of fire effects over large regions and time over large areas.
Full Citation: Lutes DC, Keane RE, Caratti JF, Key CH, Benson NC, Sutherland S, Gangi LJ (2006) FIREMON: Fire effects monitoring and inventory system. General Technical Report RMRS-GTR-164-CD. Fort Collins, CO: USDA Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station.
Developer/ Credits: Key, Carl H.; Benson, Nathan C. Agencies: USFS, US Dept. of Interior, USGS.
How applied?: Assessment of burn severity to identify high burn severity areas where active land management may be required for reforestation.

Description: Library/catalog of various climate change vulnerability assessments and relevant projects/articles. Works are summarized to include key sensitivities, broad strategies, and specific tactics to implement when confronting with a certain issue. Furthermore, they are categorized by region, keywords, climate change impacts, and resource area.
Region or Habitat: U.S., organized by US Forest Service Region
Developer/ Credits: Adaptation Partners
How applied?: Land managers could consult this library to identify relevant actions to take and things to consider for climate-adaptive post-fire management projects in the western US.
Related Publications - 1: Halofsky, J.E.; Andrews-Key, S.A.; Edwards, J.E.; Johnston, M.H.; Nelson, H.W.; Peterson, D.L.; Schmitt, K.M.; Swanston, C.W.; Williamson, T.B. (2018). Adapting forest management to climate change: The state of science and applications in Canada and the United States. Forest Ecology and Management. 421: 84–97. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foreco.2018.02.037.
Related Publications - 2: Halofsky, J.E.; Peterson, D.L. (2016). Climate change vulnerabilities and adaptation options for forest vegetation management in the northwestern USA. Atmosphere. 7: article 46. https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos7030046.
Related Publications - 3: Halofsky, J.E.; Peterson, D.L., eds. (2017). Climate change vulnerability and adaptation in the Blue Mountains. Gen. Tech. Rep. PNW-GTR-939. Portland, OR: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Pacific Northwest Research Station. https://www.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/pubs/53937.
Related Publications - 4: Halofsky, J.E.; Peterson, D.L.; Dante-Wood, S.K.; Hoang, L.; Ho, J.J.; Joyce, L.A., eds. (2018). Climate change vulnerability and adaptation in the Northern Rocky Mountains. Gen. Tech. Rep. RMRS-GTR-374. Fort Collins, CO: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station. Part 1: https://www.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/pubs/55974. Part 2: https://www.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/pubs/55975.

Description: Directs users to various online tools to help manage landscape for climate change.
Developer/ Credits: Forest Service Research and Development and the Office of Sustainability and Climate
How applied?: Provides climate change information, tools, and management-relevant resources that can be used to support adaptation and mitigation planning, evaluate climate risks, and guide implementation of climate-resilient management strategies.

Description: Integrating climate change considerations into land management.
Developer/ Credits: USDA Northern Forests Climate Hub and the Northern Institute of Applied Climate Science, a collaborative, multi-institutional partnership led by the USFS
How applied?: Provides a scalable model for collaborative climate change response across large and diverse landscapes, can support the development and application of climate adaptation startegies that can be adjusted and transferred to other regions and landscape contexts.

Tool Name: Climate Impacts Group: Choosing and using climate change scenarios for ecological impacts assessments and conservation decisions
Description: Guidelines for selecting climate change scenarios in ecological impacts assessments and conservation decisions. Addresses the problem of uncertainty: With the current proliferation of climate simulations and downscaling methods, scientifically credible strategies for selecting a subset for analysis and decision making are needed.
Region or Habitat: Any region, guidelines are not geographically specific.
Full Citation: Snover AK, Mantua NJ, Littell JS, Alexander MA, McClure M, Nye J (2013) Choosing and using climate change scenarios for ecological impacts assessments and conservation decisions. Conservation Biology 27(6): 1147-1157. https://doi.org/10.1111/cobi.12163
Developer/ Credits: Snover, A.K., Mantua, N.J., Littell, J.S., Alexander, M.A., McClure, M., Nye, J, Climate Impacts Group, University of Washington.
How applied?: Guides ecological impact assessment and natural resource management by selecting climate-change scenarios to evaluate species and ecosystem responses and support uncertainty-informed climate adaptation and management decisions.

Description: Open source software package using GitHub: Provides easy access to climate data to support climate scenario planning. Enables users to navigate barriers of big data.
Region or Habitat: Focussed on continental U.S., tool is not geographically specific.
Full Citation: Tuff T, Collins M, Williams T, Joseph M, Stavros N, Verleye E (2022). Climate Futures Toolbox (1.0.0). Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.7144863
Developer/ Credits: Tuff, T., Collins, M., Williams, T.,  Joseph, M., Stavros, N., Verleye, E., Earth Lab, University of Colorado Boulder
How applied?: Supports climate scenario planning.

Description: A program to generate high-resolution climate data for climate change studies and applications in British Columbia and western North America.
Region or Habitat: British Columbia, Canada and western North America
Full Citation: Wang T, Hamann A, Spittlehouse D, Carroll C (2016) Locally Downscaled and Spatially Customizable Climate Data for Historical and Future Periods for North America. PLoS ONE 11(6): e0156720. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0156720
Developer/ Credits: Tongli Wang, Andreas Hamann, D. Spittlehouse, and Sally Aitken, Center for Forest Conservation and Genetics, University of British Columbia
How applied?: Provides site-specific climate and climate change projections that can be used to assess future changes in temperatures and precipitation and inform forest management and adaptation planning.
Related Publications - 1: “ClimateBC; modeling climates in BC forest ecosystems“, Forest Genetics Council Annual Report 2011-2012 (see page 14 in the PDF report). http://www.fgcouncil.bc.ca/FGC-AnnualReport-1112-12Dec2012-web.pdf
Related Publications - 2: Wang, T., Hamann, A., Spittlehouse, D., and Murdock, T. N. 2012. ClimateWNA – High-resolution spatial climate data for western North America. Journal of Applied Meteorology and Climatology 61: 16-29.
Related Publications - 3: Wang, T., Hamann, A., Spittlehouse, D., and Aitken, S. N. 2006. Development of scale-free climate data for western Canada for use in resource management.. International Journal of Climatology 26: 383-397.

Tool Name: Climate Impacts Group: Climate Change Scenarios
Description: PNW-Specific Climate Forecasts.
Full Citation: Mote PW, Salathé EP, Duliere V, Jump E (2008) Scenarios of Future Climate Change for the Pacific Northwest. Climate Impacts Group, Center for Science in the Earth System, Joint Institute for the Study of the Atmosphere and Oceans, University of Washington. https://doi.org/10.7915/CIG5S757F
Developer/ Credits: Mote, P.W., Salathé, E.P., Dulire, V., Jump, E. Climate Impacts Group, University of Washington.
How applied?: Informs climate adaptation planning by assesing projected temperature and precipitation trends.

Description: Localize climate data (historic data and future projections).
Region or Habitat: United States. Can select Area of Interest anywhere in the U.S.
Full Citation: Hegewisch KC, Abatzoglou JT, Chegwidden O, Nijssen B (2024) Climate mapper web tool. Climate Toolbox, pp.13-23. https://climatetoolbox.org/
Developer/ Credits: University of California Merced
How applied?: Used to visualize historical and future climate conditions to asses climate trends and support climate adaptation planning for forest, restoration, fire danger, and management decisions.

Description: A collection of tools that provide maps and graphs of climate and hydrology data so there is no need to download or process the data yourself to obtain the important information the data contain.
Developer/ Credits: University of California Merced
How applied?: Supports climate-informed management by providing a set of tools used to evaluate climate trends, assess exposure to climate stressors (e.g., drought, heat), and inform adaptation planning and management decisions across forest, agricultural, and landscape contexts.

Description: Visualizing climate change impacts on map. Interactive and accessible to both technical and non-technical audience.
Developer/ Credits: The Nature Conservancy
How applied?: Used to explore historical and projected temperature and precipitation patterns, visulaize climate change scenarios, and support climate adaptation planning decisions.

Description: Reforestation guidance for California following drought and fire tree mortality.
Developer/ Credits: Z. Steel, M. Meyer, A. Wuenschel, S. Ostoja, and M. North, USFS
How applied?: Used to prioritize reforestationareas, assess post-drought stand conditions, and guide species selection and planting startegies for climate-adapted forest management.

Tool Name: Conserving Nature's Stage: Identifying resilient terrestrial landscapes in the Pacific Northwest
Description: Landscape resilience: goal was to identify sites in the Pacific Northwest which best allow for adaptive responses by terrestrial animal species and vegetation communities to a changing climate.
Region or Habitat: Pacific Northwestn (broadly applied): WA, OR, parts of MT, CA
Full Citation: Buttrick S, Popper K, Schindel M, McRae B, Unnasch B, Jones A, Platt J (2015) Conserving Nature’s Stage: Identifying Resilient Terrestrial Landscapes in the Pacific Northwest. The Nature Conservancy, Portland Oregon. 104 pp. http://nature.ly/resilienceNW
Developer/ Credits: The Nature Conservancy
How applied?: Supports conservation planning by identifying resilient lanscapes and conectivity pathways to prioritize protection and maintain biodiversity under climate change.
Related Publications - 1: Buttrick, S., Popper, K. McRae, B., Unnasch, B., Schindel, M., Jones, A., & Platt, J. 2015. Conserving Nature's Stage: Identifying Resilient Terrestrial Landscapes in the Pacific Northwest.
Related Publications - 2: McRae, B., Popper, K., Jones, A., Schindel, M., Buttrick, S., Hall, K., Unnasch, R. & Platt, J. 2016. Conserving Nature’s Stage: Mapping Omnidirectional Connectivity for Resilient Terrestrial Landscapes in the Pacific Northwest. 10.13140/RG.2.1.4158.6166.

Description: Information on seed collection and transfer of native plants for current and future climates. Calculates and maps current and future seed transfer limits for plant species with or without genetic information using climate data generating from ClimateNA (Wang et al. 2016).
Full Citation: Richardson BA, Milano ER, White L, Ishkarin K, Stevenson-Molnar N, Joseph G, Campbell EM, Saenz-Romero C (2025) Climate Smart Restoration Tool. https://climaterestorationtool.org
Developer/ Credits: USFS, Oregon State University, Bureau of Land Management and the Conservation Biology Institute
How applied?: Guides seed transfer and restoration planning by identifying climate analogs, evaluating species shifts, and selecting climate-adapted plant materials under future climate conditions.
Related Publications - 1: Richardson, B. A., Rehfeldt, G. E., Sáenz-Romero, C., & Milano, E. R. (2024). A climate analog approach to evaluate seed transfer and vegetation transitions. Frontiers in Forests and Global Change, 7, 1325264. https://doi.org/10.3389/ffgc.2024.1325264

Description: Data Basin is a science-based mapping and analysis platform that supports learning, research, and sustainable environmental stewardship. Data Basin compliles spatial data uploaded by members: spatial datasets, galleries, case studies, and collaboration groups. Data Basin integrates and synthesizes data about conservation, climate change, and natural resources across boundaries and organizations.
Region or Habitat: Worldwide, including "North Pacific Landscape": AK, BC, WA, OR, CA, https://northpacificlcc.org/
Developer/ Credits: Conservation Biology Institute, CBI
How applied?: Provides spatial data used to identify priority conservation areas, assess climate vulnerability, evaluate connectivity, and support land protection, restoration and regional planning decicions.

Tool Name: USDA Ecological Site Description State-and-Transition Models
Description: Literature resource describing ecological sites and state-and-transition models of vegetation change and disturbance response.
Full Citation: Stringham TK, Novak-Echenique P, Blackburn P, Coombs C, Snyder D, Wartgow A (2015) Final Report for USDA Ecological Site Description State-and-Transition Models, Major Land Resource Area 28A and 28B Nevada. University of Nevada Reno, Nevada Agricultural Research Report 2015-01. p. 1524.
Developer/ Credits: Stringham et al., USDA Natural Resource Conservation Service, USDA Agricultural Research Service, USDI Bureau of Land Management and Eureka County, NV
How applied?: Used to support rangeland assessment, restoration, and adaptive management by identifying vegetation states, evaluating disturbance responses and ecological thresholds, and guiding restoration actions needed to transition sites toward desired ecological conditions.

Description: Tool helps communities identify a set of strategies which are tailored to their strengths and needs, and based on practices which have been successful in communities similar to theirs.
Developer/ Credits: Fire Adapted Communities Learning Network and Dr. Travis Paveglio
How applied?: An action guide to facilitate your next steps in improving community wildfire resilliancy.
Tool Development Opportunities: Database on case studies and a map showing where this tool has been used.
Related Publications - 1: Travis B Paveglio, Annie Schmidt, Michelle Medley-Daniel, The Fire Adapted Communities Pathways Tool: Facilitating Social Learning and a Science of Practice, Journal of Forestry, Volume 122, Issue 2, March 2024, Pages 194–205, https://doi.org/10.1093/jofore/fvad044

Tool Name: Fire Adapted Communities Learning Network - Resources
Description: A library of resources and tools, organized by a framework on Fire Adapted Communities.
Region or Habitat: Worldwide, mostly focused on the U.S.
Developer/ Credits: Watershed Research and Training Center (developer) and Fire Adapted Communities Learning Network (site manager)
How applied?: Supports wildfire resilience by providing tools, training, and collaborative networks to reduce wildfire risks, implement mitigation actions, and develop long-term fire-adapted community strategies.

Description: Supports users in performing a variety of calculations related to fire behavior and emissions: Fuel and Fire Tools (FFT) is a software application that integrates several fire management tools, including the Fuel Characteristic Classification System (FCCS – version 4.0), Consume (version 5.0), Fire Emission Production Simulator (FEPS – version 2.0), Pile Calculator, and Digital Photo Series (DPS) into a single user interface.
Developer/ Credits: USFS, Pacific Northwest Research Station
How applied?: Used to support fire and fuel planning by estimating fuelbed characteristics, carbon storage and emmisions, and fire behavior.

Description: Immediate Fire Effects/Tree Mortality: a computer program for predicting tree mortality, fuel consumption, smoke production, and soil heating caused by prescribed fire or wildfire.
Region or Habitat: U.S., Pacific West, Interior West, North East, and South East
Full Citation: U.S. Forest Service, Missoula Fire Sciences Laboratory (2020) FOFEM fire effects model. https://research.fs.usda.gov/firelab/products/dataandtools/fofem/spatialfofem-fire-effects-model
Developer/ Credits: Missoula Fire Sciences Laboratory, Sharon M. Hood, USFS
How applied?: Used to guide prescribed fire planning, estimate emissions, assess fire effects, and inform post-fire timber salvage and fuels management decisions.

Description: Forest management and scenario planning in Oregon and Washington. Visualize alternative management scenarios on user's lands and receive immediate feedback on how decisions might pay off (timber harvest, carbon sequestration, ecosystem services).
Developer/ Credits: Ecotrust
How applied?: Supports forest planning by comparing management scenarios and evaluating treatment trade-offs, such as harvest and thinning, to inform long-term forest management decisions.

Tool Name: Suggested Stocking Levels for Forest Stands in Northeastern Oregon and Southeastern Washington
Description: Guide for number of trees per acre and species to plant after harvest or fire based on forest type.
Full Citation: Cochran PH, Geist JM, Clemens DL, Clausnitzer RR, Powell DC (1993) Suggested stocking levels for forest stands in northeastern Oregon and southeastern Washington. Res. Note PNW-RN-513. Portland, OR: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Pacific Northwest Research Station. 26 p
Developer/ Credits: USFS, typically writteen at the forest-level
How applied?: Select stocking density based on management objectives. A forest returns after harvest, fire or other disturbancce.
Tool Development Opportunities: Implement climate effects on forest demographic process (e.g., seedling survival) to better estimate stocking density under warmer, drier climates.

Description: Satellite-Based Change Recognition and Tracking. ForWarn III is a satellite-based monitoring and assessment tool that provides a near-real-time national overview of potential forest disturbances to direct attention and resources to locations where forest behavior seems unusual or abnormal. The satellite imagery is interpreted and delivered through the web-based Forest Change Assessment Viewer, a tool that provides an 6, and 8-day coast-to-coast snapshot of the US landscape and produces geographically relevant maps. The tool is intended to complement and focus efforts of existing forest monitoring programs.
Developer/ Credits: Eastern Forest Environmental Threat Assessment Center, USFS, Southern Research Station, Asheville, NC. https://forestthreats.org/
How applied?: Detect and monitor forest disturbances and identify areas with unusual vegetation conditions to support rapid response.

Tool Name: Future scenarios - training manual for facilitators
Description: A manual that presents training for facilitators to lead participants in assessing the outcomes of alternative scenarios.
Full Citation: Nemarundwe N, Jong W, Cronkleton P (2003) Future Scenarios as an instrument for forest management. Center for International Forestry Research.
Developer/ Credits: Center for International Forestry Research
How applied?: Support planning and training by using scenario methods to help managers and stakeholders define objectives, identify constrains, and develop strategies for managing uncertainty in future forest management projects.

Description: A suite of software programs, specifically a forest growth simulation model that predits how forest vegetation will change in response to natural succession, disturbances, proposed management actions, and how such changes affect ecosystem values.
Full Citation: Dixon GE (2002) Essential FVS: A user’s guide to the Forest Vegetation Simulator. Internal Rep. Fort Collins, CO: U. S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Forest Management Service Center. 240p.
Developer/ Credits: USFS
How applied?: Simulate forest growth and disturbance effects to support forest planning.

Description: Compilation of projects that introduce users to Human Ecology Mapping: the practice of mapping social characteristics of ecosystems.
Full Citation: U.S. Forest Service, Pacific Northwest Research Station (2016) Human Ecology Mapping Tool. https://research.fs.usda.gov/pnw/products/dataandtools/human-ecology-mapping-tool.
Developer/ Credits: USFS, USDA
How applied?: Provides support for forest and rangeland planning by engaging the public in collaboative mapping to capture human values and uses, informing decisions such as forest plan revisions, recreation planning and ecossystem service trade-offs.

Description: A synthesis, summary, and descision chart of post-fire hillslope treatment effectiveness
Full Citation: Robichaud P.R., Ashmun L.E. Sims B.D. (2010). Post-fire treatment effectiveness for hillslope stabilization. Gen Tech. Rep. RMRS-GTR-240. Fort Collins, CO: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station. 62 p.
Developer/ Credits: Robchaud et al. 2010
How applied?: Summary chart and website that guides user through post-fire hillslope stabilization treatment effectiveness

Description: GIS Mapping layers for Vegetation, Fuel, Fire, Disturbance, Topography.
Developer/ Credits: USDA, USFS, DOI, USGS, The Nature Conservancy, USDA Forest Service Rocky Mountain Research Station,  USDA Forest Service Forest Inventory and Analysis
How applied?: Used to support wildfire and land management decisions, such as fuels treatment planning, wildfire risk assessment, prescribed fire planning, and habitat and vegetation management.
Related Publications - 1: Strand, Eva K., KoriBlankenship, CoreyGucker, MarkBrunson, and EugénieMontBlanc. 2025. “Changing Fire Regimes in the Great Basin USA.” Ecosphere16(2): e70203. https://doi.org/10.1002/ecs2.70203
Related Publications - 2: McKerrow, A., Davidson, A., Rubino, M., Faber-Langendoen, D., & Dockter, D. (2021). Quantifying the Representation of Plant Communities in the Protected Areas of the U.S.: An Analysis Based on the U.S. National Vegetation Classification Groups. Forests, 12(7), 864. https://doi.org/10.3390/f12070864
Related Publications - 3: Sohl, T., Schleeweis, K., Herold, N., Lang, M., Puma, I. P. L., Wickham, J., Mueller, R., Rigge, M., Dewitz, J., Brown, J. F., Ingebritsen, J., Ellenwood, J., Wengert, E., Rowe, J., Flanagan, P., Kachergis, E., Garthwaite, I., & Wu, Z. (2025). An interagency perspective on improving consistency and transparency of land use and land cover mapping (No. 1549). U.S. Geological Survey. https://doi.org/10.3133/cir1549
Related Publications - 4: Vanderhoof MK, Menick CE, Picotte JJ, Robertson KM, Nowell HK, Matechik C, Hawbaker TJ. (2025) Modelling and mapping burn severity of prescribed and wildfires across the southeastern United States (2000–2022). International Journal of Wildland Fire 34, WF24137. https://doi.org/10.1071/WF24137

Tool Name: Mapping, Prioritization, and Public Participation (M3P) Tool
Description: Provides land managers with an understanding of public engagement, communication, and relations to connect public participation to wildfire planning.
Developer/ Credits: USFS, RMRS FFS
How applied?: Surveying public perception of landscapes (ecosystem services) pre-fire to facilitate post-fire management.

Description: Dynamic Vegation Model to predict changes in vegetation cover, carbon and nitrogen cycling, water budget due to climate change.
Full Citation: Bachelet D (2015) MC2 Dynamic Global Vegetation Model. https://databasin.org/articles/8e01bd5f101c4863a0d6a69d58c9298b/
Developer/ Credits: Dominique Bachelet
How applied?: Assess wildfire occurrence, impacts, and emissions to prioritize fuel treatments, evaluate post-fire succession to guide restoration strategies, and support long-term planning for fire-prone landscapes under climate change.
Related Publications - 1: Bachelet, D., and D. Turner (editors). 2015. Global Vegetation Dynamics: Concepts and Applications in the MC1 Model. AGU Geophysical Monographs 214. 210pp
Related Publications - 2: Bachelet, D., K. Ferschweiler, T.J. Sheehan, B.M. Sleeter and Z. Zhu. 2015. Projected carbon stocks in the conterminous US with land use and variable fire regimes. Global Change Biology 21(12): 4548-4560. DOI: 10.1111/gcb.13048.
Related Publications - 3: Peterman, W., D. Bachelet, K. Ferschweiler, and T. Sheehan. 2014. Soil depth affects simulated carbon and water in the MC2 dynamic global vegetation model. Ecological Modelling 294:84-93.

Tool Name: Monitoring Trends in Burn Severity (MTBS) Program
Description: Fire and burn severity mapping. An interagency program whose goal is to consistently map the burn severity and extent of large fires across Alllands of the United States from 1984 to present. This includes Allfires 1,000 acres or greater in the western United States and 500 acres or greater in the eastern Unites States.
Region or Habitat: Continental U.S., Alaska, Hawaii and Puerto Rico.
Developer/ Credits: USFS, USDA, DOI, USGS
How applied?: Developed primarily to monitor the effectiveness of national fire management policies, MTBS data are also is used to assess the immediate and long term effects of fires on the landscape, as a consistently derived source of fire disturbance data used by national land cover and monitoring programs, as a supplemental information tool to inform operational fire suppression decisions by wildfire managers, and provides a rich and unprecedented dataset for analysis and application by the research community.
Related Publications - 1: Finco, Mark; Quayle, Brad; Zhang, Yuan; Lecker, Jennifer; Megown, Kevin A.; Brewer, C. Kenneth. 2012. Monitoring Trends and Burn Severity (MTBS): Monitoring wildfire activity for the past quarter century using landsat data. In: Morin, RandAllS.; Liknes, Greg C., comps. Moving from status to trends: Forest Inventory and Analysis (FIA) symposium 2012; 2012 December 4-6; Baltimore, MD. Gen. Tech. Rep. NRS-P-105. Newtown Square, PA: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Northern Research Station. [CD-ROM]: 222-228.

Description: Provides downscaled climate projections for temperature and precipitation, and vizualization of maps, time series, and summaries of historical and future predictions.
Region or Habitat: U.S. (State, County, or Hydrologic Unit Level Climate Projections)
Developer/ Credits: USGS
How applied?: Supports climate adaptation planning by assessing changes in temperature, precipitation, and water balance to inform watershed management, forestry, and conservation decisions.

Description: A collaborative network that connects climate change education, outreach, and research across extension programs to support communication, knowledge exchange, coordination, and collaborative project development.
Developer/ Credits: Paul Lachapelle (Montana State University), Western Rural Development Center
How applied?: Supports coordination of climate change education and outreach across programs, enabling shared resources, collaborative grand development, and sharing climate adaption guidance to land managers and communities.

Description: Geospatial products depicting the probability of high-severity fire, if it were to occur, for several ecoregion in the contiguous western US.
Full Citation: Parks SA, Holsinger LM, Panunto MH, Jolly WM, Dobrowski SZ, and Dillon GK (2018) High-severity fire: evaluating its key drivers and mapping its probability across western US forests. Environmental Research Letters. 13: 044037.
Developer/ Credits:
Sean Parks, Aldo Leopold Wilderness Research Institute
USFS, Rocky Mountain Research Station
How applied?: Provides maps of high-severity fire probability that can help prioritize fuel treatments and support landscape-scale wildfire risk reduction and forest management.

Tool Name: Northern Institute of Applied Climate Science
Description: Software, online tools, data, and applied resources developed through collaboration to support climate adaptation and carbon management.
Developer/ Credits: USFS
How applied?: Supports climate adaptation and carbon management by providing technical guidance and training to inform ecosystem planning, guide management actions, and improve resilience under changing climate conditions.

Tool Name: Northwest Fire Science Consortium Tools Database
Description: Website with large list of fire management related tools.
Region or Habitat: Northwestern U.S., with some tools for U.S. and beyond
Developer/ Credits: Oregon State University, University of Oregon, Ecossystem Workforce Program, USFS, Washington State University
How applied?: Search and understand available tools for manageing natural resources across the northwestern US.
Tool Development Opportunities: Tools can be grouped into categories and linked to the appropriate spatial scale and extent for usage

Description: Geographic Area Coordination Center for the Northwest Region serves as the focal point for interagency resource coordination, logistics support, aviation support and predictive services for Allstate and federal agencies involved in wildland fire management and suppression in the region.
Developer/ Credits: BLM, USFS, USFWS, USDA,  NPS, ODF, WA DNR (Cooperating agencies include the: Bureau of Land Management, US Forest Service, Oregon Dept of Forestry, US Fish and Wildlife Service, Bureau of Indian Affairs, Washington Dept. of Natural Resources and the National Park Service)
How applied?: Coordination, fire suppression, information portal of what occurs at a fire; Improved coordination of various management agencies.
Tool Development Opportunities: Information on how to best use Allthe various tools/information on this website (very resourceful!)

Description: Natural resources digital library, a geospatial application programming interface for analyzing, displaying, and downloading environmental data.
Developer/ Credits: Oregon State University and Institute for Natural Resources.
How applied?: Find, retrieve geospatial data, overlay several data sources, access additional (non-spatial) data, such as photos, charts, tables, etc. to improve management decisions.

Description: The PFI is a categorization of post-fire soil status using a literature review. Five broad classes were identified in Jain et. al. (2012) indicating the % soil organic matter left post-fire; broad classes were subdivided based on post-fire mineral soil colors.
Region or Habitat: Temperate mixed conifer forests; best suited for western USA
Full Citation: Jain TB, Pilliod DS, Graham RT, Lentile LB, Sandquist JE (2012) Index for Characterizing Post-Fire Soil Environments in Temperate Coniferous Forests. Forests, 3(3), 445-466. https://doi.org/10.3390/f3030445
Developer/ Credits: USFS, USGS, University of the South, Sewanee
How applied?: Identifying PFI could help land managers understand the nutrient levels of the soil and plan reforestation efforts accordingly. E.g., if the PFI indicates that the soils are nutrient-poor, then soil ammendments such as mulch may important in restoration.
Tool Development Opportunities: Expand to other types of forests, shrublands, etc.

Description: Prototype decision tree designed to designed to assess the feasibility of a streamlined NEPA process in support of potential post-fire salvage projects.
Developer/ Credits: Paul Hessburg (USFS), PNW Research Station
How applied?: Recommendation - ecological restoration vs. fiber recovery, generated within the short time window appropriate to salvage operations.

Description: Visualization of observed phenology data.
Developer/ Credits: National Phenology Network
How applied?: Tracks plant and animal activity timing with variables such as latitude, temperature, and precipitation, which can inform restoration timing and wildlife monitoring.

Tool Name: A guidebook to spatial datasets for conservation planning under climate change in the Pacific Northwest
Description: Spatial datasets for conservation planning  under climate change in the Pacific Northwest.
Full Citation: Cartwright JM (2020) A guidebook to spatial datasets for conservation planning under climate change in the Pacific Northwest. U.S. Geological Survey. https://doi.org/10.5066/P92L1H7O.
Developer/ Credits: Cartwright J.M., USGS, NWCASC
How applied?: Provides information on spatial datasets that can be applied to climate projections, changes in hydrologic conditions, vegetation and fire-regime shifts, animal habitat changes, species movements, and topographic and soil conditions relevant to climate change.
Tool Development Opportunities: Datasets could be combined and made available throught a data portal

Tool Name: Postfire Restoration Framework for National Forests in California
Description: Science-based framework to guide postfire restoration on national forests in California. Framework is based on a set of restoration ecology principles and presents a five-step, structured process to develop a restoration portfolio that can inform monitoring and planning. Also, presentation of five tools/ tool types (as appendices).
Full Citation: Meyer MD, Long JW, Safford HD (2021) Postfire restoration framework for national forests in California (PSW-GTR-270; p. PSW-GTR-270). U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Pacific Southwest Research Station. https://doi.org/10.2737/PSW-GTR-270
Developer/ Credits: USFS (Pacific Southwest Research Station) (Meyer, M.D.; Long, J.W.; Safford, H.D., eds.)
How applied?: Develop landscape-scale post-fire (and related) restoration plans, based on a science-based process. Inform monitoring. Predict post-fire conifer regeneration at landscape scale.
Tool Development Opportunities: Regarding the case studies, expand the variety of example ecosystems (e.g., moist mixed conifer forest type)

Description: A decision-making tool that helps resource managers make informed strategies for responding to ecological changes resulting from climate change.
Developer/ Credits: Gregor Schuurman (NPS), David Cole (USFS), Amanda Cravens (USGS), and many others, including individuals associated with the Federal Navigating Ecological Transformation (FedNET) working group, The American Fisheries Society, and The Wildlife Society’s Ecosystem Transformation working group
How applied?: Guides managers in selecting strategies to resist, accept, or direct ecosystem change under climate change. Could be used to support restoration, invasive species management, and wildfire adaptation.
Related Publications - 1: Lynch, A. J., Ashander, J. D., Ciocco, T. W., Cravens, A. E., Dassow, C. J., Dee, L. E., Dunham, J. B., Eaton, M. J., Embke, H. S., Hennessy, J., Latzka, A. W., Lawrence, D. J., Littell, J. S., Miller, B. W., Palasti, L. A., Runge, M. C., Sass, G. G., Shultz, A. D., Siegel, K. J., … Yocum, H. M. (2025). RAD (Resist-Accept-Direct) switch points and triggers for adaptation planning. Journal of Environmental Management, 392, 126419. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2025.126419
Related Publications - 2: Sesser, A. L, Wilkening, J. L, Miles, W. B., Guilbeau, K., Lynch, A. J., Conrad, J. R., & Magness, D. R. (2025). Integrating the resist–accept–direct framework into natural resource decision-making processes for climate adaptation. Conservation Biology, 39, e70116. https://doi.org/10.1111/cobi.70116
Related Publications - 3: Cravens, A. E., Clifford, K. R., Knapp, C., & Travis, W. R. (2025). The dynamic feasibility of resisting (R), accepting (A), or directing (D) ecological change. Conservation Biology, 39, e14331. https://doi.org/10.1111/cobi.14331
Related Publications - 4: Ward, N.K., Lynch, A.J., Beever, E.A. et al. Reimagining large river management using the Resist–Accept–Direct (RAD) framework in the Upper Mississippi River. Ecol Process 12, 48 (2023). https://doi.org/10.1186/s13717-023-00460-x

Tool Name: Rapid Assessment of Vegetation Conditions after Wildfire (RAVG)
Description: Rapid assessment of vegetation conditions post-fire using Landsat imagery to calculate change in Relative differenced normalized burn ratio (RdNBR) for fires that burned at least 1,000 acres of national forest land.
Developer/ Credits: USFS, US Dept. of Interior, USGS
How applied?: Identify areas where active land management may be needed to rehabilitate vegetation conditions to a desirable state.

Description: A web-based decision support system was developed (DSS) to provide spatial information on potential for natural regeneration in post-disturbance environments. Users can select burn severity information using 1-year postfire data (Monitoring Trends in Burn Severity), or for recent disturbance events using either Rapid Assessment of Vegetation Condition After Wildfire or Soil Burn Severity Layers.
Full Citation: Holden ZA, Warren D, Davis K, Jungck E, Maneta M, Dobrowski S, Archer V (2022) REGEN MAPPER: A web-based tool for predicting postfire conifer regeneration and prioritizing reforestation efforts in the western United States. In: Jain TB, Schuler TM [comp.]. Foundational concepts in silviculture with emphasis on reforestation and early stand improvement - 2022 National Silviculture Workshop. Proc. RMRS-P-80. Fort Collins, CO: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station. 2 p.
Developer/ Credits: Holden, Z.A., et al.
How applied?: The RegenMapper tool provides a means for forest managers to assess and prioritize sites for planting following high severity wildfire or other disturbance.
Tool Development Opportunities: Add in burn severity rasters from GEE so user does not have to upload.
Related Publications - 1: Holden ZA, Jungck E, Davis KT, Warren DA, Swanson A, Dobrowski SZ, Maneta M, Rodman KC, Faller L, Archer V. A post-fire reforestation assessment and prioritization tool for the Western United States. Fire Ecol. 2025;21(1):83. doi: 10.1186/s42408-025-00405-z. Epub 2025 Dec 11. PMID: 41395376; PMCID: PMC12698830.

Description: A synthesis of post-fire road treatment information based on expert information and interviews of BAER team engineers
Full Citation: Foltz, R.B.; Robichaud, P.R.; Rhee, H. 2009. A synthesis of post-fire road treatments for BAER teams: methods, treatment effectiveness, and decisionmaking tools for rehabilitation. Gen. Tech. Rep. RMRS-GTR-228. Fort Collins, CO: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station. 152 p.
Developer/ Credits: USFS RMRS , Foltz et al. 2009
How applied?: Develop post-fire road treatments

Tool Name: Potential Ecosystem Resilience and Resistance in Sagebrush Habitat
Description: GIS Raster Layer: Resistance & resilience in sagebrush.
Developer/ Credits: BLM, Fire and Invasive Assessment Tool (FIAT)
How applied?: Provides resilience and resistance maps, can help prioritize fuel treatments, invasive species control, and sagebrush restoration to reduce wildfire risk and support sage-grouse habitat conservation.

Description: Assists BAER (burn area emergency response) teams assess post-fire watershed conditions by providing insights regarding how to categorize soil burn severity using soil condition indicators.
Full Citation: Parsons A, Robichaud PR, Lewis SA, Napper C, Clark JT (2010) Field guide for Mapping Port-Fire Soil Burn Severity. Gen. Tech. Rep. RMRS-GTR-243. Fort Collins, CO: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station. 49 p.
Developer/ Credits: Annette Parsons, Peter Robichaud, Sarah Lewis, Carolyn Napper, Jess Clark; USDA RMRS
How applied?: Identify areas where soil burn severity indicates high severity burn as potential locations that will require active management projects to facilitate post-fire recovery.
Tool Development Opportunities: Highlight tool through more outlets to make it easier for individuals outside of federal agencies to find

Tool Name: Scenarios as a tool for adaptive forest management: A guide.
Description: A guide that presents the principles and methods of scenario-based management. It describes how scenarios can be used in adaptive forest management and defines and exhibits the utility of considering alternative scenarios in management.
Region or Habitat: Global, but focussed on tropical forest communities
Full Citation: Wollenburg E, Edmunds D, Buck L (2000) Scenarios as a tool for adaptive forest management: A guide. Center for International Forestry Research.
Developer/ Credits: Eva Wollenberg with David Edmunds and Louise Buck; Center for International Forestry Research
How applied?: Used to support adaptive forest management through scenario planning, collaborative decision-making, risk assessment, monitoring priorizitation, and preparation for future environmental and social change.

Description: Get data on seed zones for use in plant material development, gene conservation and native plant restoration activities.
Developer/ Credits: Western Wildlands Environmental Threats Assessment Center (WWETAC)
How applied?: Allows user to match climate conditions for two areas to select best available seed source.
Tool Development Opportunities: Currently, focused on SW US and non-arboreal vegetation types (shrubs, forbs, grasses)

Description: Pairs with Seedlot Selection Tool, but allows users to look at how habitat suitability will change with climate change.
Developer/ Credits: Glenn Howe (Oregon State University), Brad St. Clair (USFS, Pacific Northwest Research Station), Ron Beloin (Oregon State University), Dominique Bachelet (Conservation Biology Institute), Nikolas Stevenson-Molnar (Conservation Biology Institute), Brendan Ward (Conservation Biology Institute)
How applied?: Used to help forest managers identify species and vegetation types suitable for specific sites under different climate change scenarios, and to support the transition toward forest compositions that are better adapted to future climatic conditions.

Description: Web-based tool that identifies climatically and topographically suitable sites to plant ponderosa pine and Douglas-fir seedlings after wildfire.
Region or Habitat: North America, species: PICO, PSME, PIPO, PIEN, LAOC, PIMO, PISI
Full Citation: Rodman K, Fornwalt P, Chapman T, Coop J, Edwards G, Stevens J, Veblen T (2022) SRRT: A Decision Support Tool to Inform Postfire Reforestation of Ponderosa Pine and Douglas-fir in the Southern Rocky Mountains. Res. Note RMRS-RN-95. Fort Collins, CO: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station. 12 p. https://doi. org/10.2737/RMRS-RN-95
Developer/ Credits: Kyle Rodman (Ecological Restoration Institute), Paula Fornwalt (Rocky Mountain Research Station), Teresa Chapman (The Nature Conservancy), Jonathan Coop (Western Colorado University), Gloria Edwards (Southern Rockies Fire Science Network), Jens Stevens (USFS), Thomas Veblen (University of Colorado Boulder)
How applied?: Identifies the best tree species to plant based on future climate conditions. Prioritizes areas for post-fire planting that minimize costs (near roads) and increase the chance for seedling survival based on future climate.
Tool Development Opportunities: Integrate more tree species, seed lots, and larger geographic area

Description: Web-based mapping application that matches seedlots to current and future climate conditions at planting sites.
Developer/ Credits: Glenn Howe (OSU), Brad St.Clair (USFS), Nikolas Stevenson-Molnars and others (Conservation Biology Institute )
How applied?: Identify an appropriate seedlot for a planting site based on future climate conditions. Adapt forest at planting site to projected future climate conditions (multiple scenarios).
Tool Development Opportunities: Finer spatial resolution DEM (30m?). Rather than individual species, maybe it could Recommendgroups of species

Tool Name: Success Stories, Forests and Rangelands (Fire Adapted Communities Case Studies)
Description: Filterable list of success stories. Forests and Rangelands highlights land management effort successes and project information including wildland fire fighting, landscape restoration and rehabilitation, fuels reduction, community assistance, and related land management activities.
Developer/ Credits: Various federal agencies and partners.
How applied?: Inform management decisions based on successes elsewhere.

Description: A software to create and analyze survey results with easy implementation for maps into surveys.
Developer/ Credits: ESRI
How applied?: A platform for standardized, georeferenced field data collection that can be used to document management actions and track restoration progress.

Tool Name: Template for Assessing Climate Change Impacts and Management Options (TACCIMO)
Description: Connects climate change science with forest management and planning needs.
Developer/ Credits: USDA, USFS
How applied?: Used to support forest planning and management by providing access to climate change impacts literature and management options, helping assess climate risks, identifying adaptation strategies, and generating reports to aid forest planning and management analysis.

Tool Name:
Terrestrial Condition Assessment 
(Only available in the USFS)
Description: Evaluate landscapes to indentify stressors and opportunities for restoration.
Full Citation: Cleland D, Reynolds K, Vaughan R, Schrader B, Li H, Laing L (2017) Terrestrial condition assessment for national forests of the USDA Forest Service in the continental US. Sustainability. 9(12): 2144-. https://doi.org/10.3390/su9112144
Developer/ Credits: USFS
How applied?: Used to evaluate ecological integrity and disturbance impacts across national forest landscapes, identify priority land-type associations for restoration, and support strategic and tactical planning by synthesizing multiple national datasets.

Tool Name: Threat-based Land Management in the Northern Great Basin: A Manager’s Guide
Description: Sagebrush ecosystem management - this guide (32 pp.) presents a simplified framework to help land managers assess and monitor the threats of encroachment by juniper and invasive grasses (vegetation state transition) at large scales.
Region or Habitat: Northern Great Basin (Nevada, Utah, S. Oregon)
Full Citation: Johnson D, Cahill M, Boyd C, Schroeder V, Foster L, Sitz A, Kerby J, Svejcar TJ, Cupples J (2019) Threat-Based Land Management in the Northern Great Basin: A Manager’s Guide. Pacific Northwest Extension Publishing. https://extension.oregonstate.edu/sites/extd8/files/documents/pnw722.pdf
Developer/ Credits: Johnson D. et. al. 2009, SageSHARE working group; key partners: Oregon State University, USDA Agricultural Research Service, The Nature Conservancy, US Fish and Wildlife Service, Oregon Dept. of Fish and Wildlife
How applied?: Land managers identify ecosystem of interest and determine potential consequences of prescribed fire or logging practices.
Related Publications - 1: Johnson, D., Cahill, M., Boyd, C., Schroeder, V., Foster, L., Sitz, A., Kerby, J., Svejcar, T. J., & Cupples, J. (2019). Threat-Based Land Management in the Northern Great Basin: A Field Guide. Pacific Northwest Extension Publishing. https://extension.oregonstate.edu/sites/extd8/files/documents/pnw723.pdf

Tool Name: Values at Risk (VAR) BAER Calculation Rule
Description: Cost-risk analysis worksheet to identifiy and quantify values-at-risk (VAR) from the secondary effects of wildfire
Full Citation: Calkin, D.E., Hyde, K.D., Robichaud, P.R., Jones, J.G., Ashmun, L.E. and Dan, L., 2007. Assessing post-fire values-at-risk with a new calculation tool. Gen. Tech. Rep. RMRS-GTR-205. Fort Collins, CO: US Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station. 32 p., 205.
Developer/ Credits: Calkin et al. 2007. Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station
How applied?: Post-fire assessment to identify and evaluate values-at-risk (VAR), such as human life and safety, cultural and ecological resources, land use, and existing infrastructure, from potential secondary effectsof wildfires, examples include: increased runoff, erosion, flooding, sedimentation, and vulnerability to invasive weeds.

Description: An interactive, web-based decision tool published online. Designed to inform the establishment of a regional framework that would provide the basis for an alternative, rapid NEPA process that could support ecologically sound post-fire logging during the first winter after wildfire.
Region or Habitat: Not specified; tool has been applied to the Blue Mountains region (OR/WA)
Developer/ Credits: BayesFusion LLC/ Keith Reynolds (USFS)
How applied?: Alternative, rapid NEPA analysis, to support  ecologically sound post-fire logging during the first winter after wildfire.

Tool Name: Water Supply Stress Index (WaSSI) Ecosystem Services Model
Description: Water Supply Stress modelling based on various landscape, climate, vegation inputs.
Region or Habitat: Continental U.S. and Mexico, Rwanda, and Burundi.
Full Citation: Caldwell, P., Sun, G., McNulty, S., Myers, J.M., Cohen, E., Herring, R. and Martinez, E., 2013. WaSSI Ecosystem Services Model [online]
Developer/ Credits: USDA, USFS, Caldwell et al. 2013
How applied?: Used to evaluate tradeoffs in water supply and carbon sequestration by simulating climate, land cover, and human use scenarios to support watershed and landscape planning.
Related Publications - 1: Duarte, H.F., Kim, J.B., Sun, G., McNulty, S., Xiao, J. (2024) Climate and vegetation change impacts on future Conterminous United States water yield. Journal of Hydrology 639: 131472. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhydrol.2024.131472

Description: A physically-based soil erosion prediction technology.
Developer/ Credits: Pete Robichaud, USFS RMRS Air, Water, and Aquatics Environments
How applied?: Supports conservation and land management planning by simulating erosion, hydrology, and vegetation responses to evaluate the effectiveness and risk of management practices under varying climate and management scenarios.

Description: A web-based resource that provides spatially and temporally explicit information regarding fire weather, fire probability, drought indicies (inlcuding live fuel moisture).
Developer/ Credits: Developed by USFS Fire Sciences Laboratory in 1994; operation shifted to National Interagency Fire Center
How applied?: Investigate current fire danger warning in your location. Identify areas and times where wildfire is most likely to occur.
Tool Development Opportunities: Revise to include utility of spatial products and shift away from point estimates.

Description: Assessing and communicating wildfire risk using maps, data, and resources to support community planning and risk reduction.
Developer/ Credits: USFS
How applied?: Assess community wildfire risk, prioritize areas for mitigation, identify vulnerable populations and high-risk locations, and support the implementation of wildfire risk reduction and community preparedness.

Description: A spatially explicit and specific wind model for use in wildland fire modeling.
Developer/ Credits: USFS, RMRS
How applied?: Land managers use for prediction of wind patterns during wildfire, identifying particularly susceptible locations for the fire to spread.
Related Publications - 1: Seto, Daisuke; Jones, Charles; Siuta, David; Wagenbrenner, Natalie; Thompson, Callum; Quinn, Nathan. 2025. Evaluation of HRRR wind speed forecast and WindNinja downscaling accuracy during Santa Ana wind events in Southern California. Weather and Forecasting. 40: 525-541.
Related Publications - 2: Wagenbrenner, Natalie; Atwood, Loren; Forthofer, Jason; Grenfell, Isaac. 2024. Predicting terrain-induced wind turbulence for smokejumper parachute operations. International Journal of Wildland Fire. 33: WF24112.
Related Publications - 3: Wagenbrenner, Natalie S.; Forthofer, Jason M.; Page, Wesley G.; Butler, Bret W. 2019. Development and evaluation of a Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes solver in WindNinja for operational wildland fire applications. Atmosphere. 10: 672.q
Related Publications - 4: Forthofer, Jason M.; Butler, Bret W.; Wagenbrenner, Natalie S. 2014. A comparison of three approaches for simulating fine-scale surface winds in support of wildland fire management: Part I. Model formulation and comparison against measurements. International Journal of Wildland Fire. doi: https://doi.org/10.1071/WF12089.

Description: Web-based soil information platform that provides access to soil maps and survey data.
Developer/ Credits: USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service
How applied?: Used to support land and conservation planning by providing soil data for suitability assessments, erosion control, restoration, and infrastructure and resource management decisions.
